methodology: Gap-length OLS regression (continuous IV) replacing binary fallow/continuous
improvement_over_s189: S189 binary classification produced continuous_n=0. Gap regression uses continuous variable.
interpretation: Gap coefficient β=0.010454 (t=2.14) in era-controlled model. Pearson r=0.1362. Positive gap→Sharpe relationship supports fallow hypothesis.
expect: Gap-length regression over domain-tagged lessons (N>>50) shows either positive correlation (gap predicts higher Sharpe, confirming fallow effect) or no correlation (falsifying B-FAR2). Effect size reported.
actual: FALLOW_CONFIRMED at n=420 (15x S189 sample). Gap beta=0.010 (t=2.14, p<0.05). Effect is statistically significant but small: +4.2% Sharpe for gap>4 vs gap=1. Era (session number) dominates variance (t=8.53). R2=0.164 (mostly era, not gap). Domain heterogeneity: meta positive (beta=0.061, ns), nk-complexity NEGATIVE (beta=-0.060, t=-1.60). Nonlinear model shows no quadratic effect (gap_sq t=-0.24). Original S189 +28.2% was methodological artifact from binary classification on empty comparison group.
diff: Expected positive or null correlation. Got weak positive (r=0.136), statistically significant at p<0.05 but practically small. Key surprise: S189 +28.2% is 7x overestimate vs true +4.2%. Era effect 4x larger than gap effect. The fallow principle HOLDS but should not drive policy — domain rotation helps marginally, but improving methodology over time (era effect) matters much more.
